emotional_core: The quiet relief of discovering you've been doing something the hard way — and the almost embarrassing simplicity of the better path that was always there.
visual_metaphor: A person carrying buckets of water one at a time, then discovering a pipe that delivers the whole river in a single operation
key_quotes: Python loops are slow, and NumPy arrays are not.
You don't write loops. You write operations, and NumPy applies them to every element.
The embarrassing part wasn't the performance difference; it was realizing I'd spent an hour writing code that NumPy had already solved years ago.

story_frames.visual: Close-up of a laptop screen showing a Python for-loop — warm tungsten light, cursor blinking slowly, a loading spinner in the corner
Same screen, now a single NumPy line — cursor returns instantly, stark visual contrast to frame 1, clean terminal output
Abstract grid of data cells all lighting up simultaneously in a wave — not one at a time, all at once, like a signal passing through a matrix
Isolated code snippet: scores[scores > 90] — white text on dark background, nothing else in frame, maximum visual clarity
Clean 2D grid diagram, one column highlighted in accent color, labels showing data[:, 2] — technical illustration, no clutter
Person at a desk, slightly slumped, staring at a screen covered in nested loop code — relatable, self-deprecating energy, warm light
Same person, upright, looking at two clean NumPy lines on the same screen — not triumphant, just quietly satisfied
